video_title: Lawfare Daily: What the Supreme Court Said About the President's Power Over Independent Agencies
gist: Law professor Nick Bagner and Lawfare's Natalie Orpett dissect two Supreme Court decisions (Trump v. Slaughter and Trump v. Cook) that overturned Humphrey's Executor and gave the president power to remove principal officers of independent agencies at will, while carving out a bespoke exception for the Federal Reserve. Bagner argues the majority's deliberately vague use of 'subordinates' rather than the traditional officer framework opens the door to stripping removal protections from up to ~2 million federal civil servants, with destabilizing second-order effects on the federal workforce.

insights.claim: The Supreme Court's Trump v. Slaughter decision overturned Humphrey's Executor (1935) and held that the president may remove principal officers of independent agencies at will, ending Congress's long-assumed power to grant for-cause removal protection to members of multi-member commissions.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Removes a ~90-year structural guardrail on independent agencies (FTC, FCC, SEC, FERC, etc.), raising the odds that agency leadership and policy now turn over with each administration.
insights.quote: Slaughter holds that the president may remove principal officers and Humphrey's Executor is overturned.

insights.claim: The Trump administration deliberately targeted FTC Commissioner Rebecca Slaughter because the FTC was the exact agency in Humphrey's Executor, making it the cleanest vehicle to force the Supreme Court to reconsider that precedent rather than distinguish it away.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Signals the removals were a coordinated legal strategy to expand executive power, not case-by-case personnel decisions, implying more test cases are engineered deliberately.
insights.quote: if you want to challenge Humphrey's executor, the best way to ensure the Supreme Court can't easily distinguish your case is to just remove a member of the Federal Trade Commission

insights.claim: The Slaughter majority declined to give a comprehensive definition of executive power but identified four functions—rulemaking, enforcement, adjudication, and filing litigation—that qualify, and because nearly every agency performs at least one, almost no agency's members can retain removal protection under the ruling.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The four-function test is broad enough to reach virtually all regulators, so investors should treat leadership at essentially every independent agency as removable and politically exposed.
insights.quote: the four items of executive power the court identifies in this case are rule making, enforcement, adjudication, and filing lawsuits, litigation

insights.claim: The Supreme Court left open a narrow theoretical carve-out for purely advisory agencies that exercise no executive power, but Bagner says it is very hard to name any agency that actually qualifies.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The 'advisory agency' exception is more theoretical than practical, so it offers little real protection to any regulator with market-relevant authority.
insights.quote: the Supreme Court suggests that agencies that don't exercise any executive power may still be subject to removal protections, but it doesn't say much and it's very hard to come up with agencies that satisfy that prong

insights.claim: Instead of using the settled principal-officer/inferior-officer/employee framework, the Slaughter majority spoke only of the president's power to control 'subordinates,' leaving undefined who is covered and making the opinion potentially far broader than intended.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The vague 'subordinates' language is the single biggest source of legal uncertainty about how far removal power now reaches into the federal workforce.
insights.quote: It refers to subordinates and it talks about how the president needs to control any subordinates. It doesn't put any contours on who those subordinates are.

insights.claim: Justice Gorsuch's concurrence opens by framing the holding as limited to principal officers, giving textual support for the narrow reading even though the majority opinion itself reads more broadly.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The Gorsuch concurrence is the key text litigants will cite to cabin the ruling; watch whether courts anchor to it or to the broader majority language.
insights.quote: Justice Gorsuch's concurrence basically begins with a statement that says today the court holds that Congress cannot restrict the removal of principal officers

insights.claim: Bagner argues the vague 'subordinates' wording was likely a negotiated compromise because Chief Justice Roberts could not hold a majority for a narrow principal-officer-only holding, with Justices Thomas and Alito hostile to language protecting inferior officers and employees.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: If the ambiguity reflects internal Court divisions rather than a clear rule, further fracturing and unpredictable follow-on decisions are likely.
insights.quote: I think we kind of got this like cobbled together bargain of an opinion that is using the word subordinates to avoid answering the question, are inferior officers removable at will?

insights.claim: Bagner offers a political-pressure explanation for the opinion's ambiguity: that Chief Justice Roberts may be avoiding fights with the Trump administration and did not need to decide the inferior-officer question now.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Frames the Court as strategically deferential under political pressure, a lens for predicting how future executive-power cases resolve.
insights.quote: it is plausible that Chief Justice Roberts kind of wants to just tow the line and not pick any fights with the Trump administration right now

insights.claim: Bagner notes Justice Sotomayor's dissent specifically called out the majority's failure to define 'subordinates,' and since Roberts saw the dissent before finalizing, the omission of any discussion of inferior officers was not a mere oversight.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Suggests the ambiguity is intentional, increasing the chance the Court deliberately left a door open for later expansion of removal power.

insights.claim: Bagner floats a third reading in which the majority intentionally opened the door for the Trump administration to argue inferior officers and employees are removable, mirroring the Seila Law tactic of inviting the next case rather than deciding it now.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: If correct, the Court is signaling a roadmap to gut civil-service protections in a subsequent ruling, a tail risk worth tracking.
insights.quote: we've opened the door, grab the door, and we'll keep it open for you when it comes

insights.claim: Unitary executive theory holds that all executive power must be exercised by the president and anyone wielding it must be subject to his control, with the corollary that Humphrey's Executor was wrongly decided—a view the conservative legal movement began embracing around the Reagan administration.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Understanding this doctrine explains the through-line connecting decades of removal-power cases and predicts the direction of further executive-power expansion.
insights.quote: a central conclusion of unitary executive theory is that Humphrey's Executor is wrongly decided

insights.claim: The chipping away at Humphrey's Executor traces from Justice Scalia's dissent in Morrison v. Olson through the appointments of Roberts and Alito—both Reagan administration veterans—culminating in Seila Law as the biggest pivot point.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Maps the multi-decade jurisprudential path, useful for anticipating how the current Court extends the doctrine next.

insights.claim: In Seila Law the Supreme Court struck down removal protection for the single-director Consumer Financial Protection Bureau created by Dodd-Frank, but preserved two exceptions—inferior officers/employees and Humphrey's Executor multi-member commissions—declining then to overrule Humphrey's.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Seila Law is the precedent that set up Slaughter; its two exceptions are the doctrinal ground now being narrowed.

insights.claim: Collins v. Yellen extended Seila Law to another single-director agency, establishing before Slaughter that Congress generally cannot protect appointees who single-handedly direct an agency from removal.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Confirms single-director agencies were already fully exposed, leaving multi-member commissions as the last protected category that Slaughter has now stripped.

insights.claim: There are competing flavors of unitary executive theory: a broad version where any agency in the executive branch must be fully removable by the president, and a more moderate appointments-clause version tying removal power to the power to appoint principal officers.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Which flavor the Roberts Court ultimately adopts determines whether removal power stops at ~5,000 principal officers or reaches ~2 million employees.

insights.claim: In Trump v. Cook the Supreme Court held that Congress can still provide for-cause removal protection for members of the Federal Reserve, carving out the Fed even as Slaughter eliminated protection for other multi-member commissions.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The Fed's insulation from at-will presidential removal survives, preserving central-bank independence markets rely on for monetary-policy stability.
insights.quote: In Trump v. Cook, the Supreme Court effectively holds that Congress can provide for cause protections for members of the Federal Reserve.

insights.claim: Bagner says the Fed exception is fundamentally prudential—Justice Kavanaugh's concurrence reflects the view that destabilizing the Fed would crash the economy—rather than a principled doctrinal consequence of Slaughter.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Because the carve-out rests on economic-stability pragmatism rather than logic, its durability depends on the Court continuing to view Fed independence as economically indispensable.
insights.quote: it's the Fed. If we create instability in the Fed, like the economy's going to crash. Like, we're not that crazy.

insights.claim: The Court justified the Fed carve-out by analogizing to the first and second Banks of the United States, but Bagner notes research by Lev Menand shows those banks were largely private, shareholder-governed entities structurally unlike the modern Fed, making the historical analogy weak.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: A shaky historical rationale means the Fed exception could be doctrinally contested later, a low-probability but high-impact risk to central-bank independence.
insights.quote: the banks of the United States actually don't look anything like the Fed in the way it was governed. They were mostly private entities.

insights.claim: The Fed exception was foreshadowed in Trump v. Wilcox, a May shadow-docket (non-merits) decision that included roughly a paragraph noting a clear Fed exception if Humphrey's Executor were overruled, and the government did not contest the constitutionality of the Fed's protections in Cook.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Shows the Fed carve-out was pre-telegraphed and uncontested, reinforcing that markets can treat Fed independence as protected for now.

insights.claim: Bagner stresses that no other agency is clearly covered by the Fed exception—it appears to be a bespoke, one-agency carve-out—leaving uncertain whether any other independent regulator could claim similar protection.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Other economically important regulators (SEC, CFTC, FTC) get no shelter from the Fed exception, so their leadership remains politically removable.
insights.quote: It is as far as we can tell, literally an exception for the Federal Reserve. And it is unclear what other agencies, if any, might fall into that exception.

insights.claim: Bagner frames Slaughter and Cook as fundamentally a battle between Congress and the president over who has authority to design the government, which the Supreme Court has decisively resolved in the president's favor.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Signals a structural shift in separation of powers that will ripple across all executive-power disputes, not just agency removals.
insights.quote: the right way to read Slaughter and Cook is it's a battle between Congress and the president, and the Supreme Court has basically decided that the president has won the battle

insights.claim: Bagner argues that under the Necessary and Proper Clause of Article 1, Congress has the prerogative to design agencies and insulate technocratic experts from political pressure, and the Court's ruling inverts this by holding Congress cannot use that power to restrict the president's executive authority.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Reframes the constitutional stakes: the decision curtails congressional design authority, a precedent that could weaken other statutory structures Congress builds into the executive branch.
insights.quote: Congress can set up these agencies however it deems necessary because ultimately, execution has always been about the faithful execution of the laws enacted by Congress

insights.claim: Congress originally designed independent agencies (heyday roughly 1880s-1930s) with fixed 5-7 year terms, for-cause removal, expertise and geographic requirements, and bipartisan appointment caps specifically to ensure policy stability and remove politics from areas like monetary and economic regulation.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Explains what stability mechanism the ruling dismantles—gradual, cross-administration turnover designed to prevent abrupt regulatory swings.
insights.quote: Congress really wanted to ensure stability. So it didn't want these agencies to change with every incoming administration and suddenly our entire monetary policy or economic policy has shifted overnight

insights.claim: Independent agencies were structured so that a commission would comprise members appointed across multiple administrations, preventing any single president from gaining substantial control over their decision-making—the exact insulation Slaughter dismantles.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The cross-administration buffer that kept regulators like FERC and the CPSC from swinging with each election is now gone, raising regulatory volatility.

insights.claim: Under the maximalist reading of Slaughter, removal power would extend beyond roughly 5,000 principal officers to as many as 2 million federal employees, and lower courts—not the Supreme Court—will be forced to decide how far it reaches.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of workforce exposure; a maximalist outcome could reshape the entire federal civil service and the government's operational capacity.
insights.quote: It not applying to the, say, 5,000 principal officers, but to probably 2 million employees. And the courts, the lower courts are going to be asked to make that call.

insights.claim: Bagner predicts the Trump administration will file notices of supplemental authority in every pending civil-service case—citing Slaughter's 'subordinates' language while omitting the Gorsuch principal-officer limitation—to argue employees can be removed at will.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Flags an imminent, concrete litigation tactic that will spread the ruling's reach across many cases before any appellate resolution.
insights.quote: the Trump administration in every one of these cases is going to file a notice of supplemental authority saying, "Hey, the Supreme Court just said that the president has power to remove subordinates at will

insights.claim: Multiple civil-service cases are already pending that Slaughter could reshape, including Jackler (whether immigration judges can be protected by civil-service law), Commins (removal of Senior Executive Service members), and the Comey case (removal of an Assistant U.S. Attorney).
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Names the specific dockets to watch for the first signals of how broadly Slaughter's removal power will be applied.

insights.claim: Bagner predicts an inevitable split among district and appellate courts over what Slaughter means for inferior officers and employees, given the opinion's ambiguity and the high stakes.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: A circuit split makes further Supreme Court intervention likely and prolongs legal uncertainty over the civil service.

insights.claim: Bagner argues the practical damage comes not from mass firings but from making the workforce feel threatened, causing voluntary attrition; declining job-satisfaction data and his preliminary research on Schedule F show employees in vulnerable positions leaving at higher rates in anticipation of losing protection.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Attrition, not litigation, is the mechanism of harm—meaning capacity erosion will show up in workforce data long before courts rule.
insights.quote: The Trump administration, even if it takes the position that the civil service system is unconstitutional, is not going to suddenly send termination letters to all 2 million employees. But, it is making the workforce feel threatened.

insights.claim: Bagner contends the Merit Systems Protection Board is captured by the Trump administration, has a huge backlog, rarely issues reinstatement injunctions, and forces employees through slow, costly review they usually cannot afford—so most will leave rather than litigate even if removal protections are ultimately upheld.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: The procedural chokepoint means legal victories may be hollow, accelerating workforce exits regardless of the underlying constitutional question.
insights.quote: The Merit Systems Protection Board is very much captured by the Trump administration and has been pretty much ordaining a lot of what the Trump administration has done with respect to removal of federal employees.

insights.claim: Bagner warns that eroding tenure protections undermines whistleblower incentives, because federal employees who fear retaliation have no incentive to report unlawful, fraudulent, or wasteful agency behavior despite existing whistleblower protections.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Weaker whistleblower activity means less internal check on agency misconduct, a governance-quality deterioration with downstream oversight implications.
insights.quote: If I'm a federal employee and I observe illegal behavior and I know I can be retaliated against if I report that behavior, I have no incentive to do so.

insights.claim: Bagner argues the federal government's reputation as a stable employer is 'shot,' and since stability is the rational trade-off for below-market pay, rebuilding agency capacity after reductions in force will be difficult even as some agencies concede they cut too deep and need to rehire.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: A damaged employer brand plus below-market pay implies persistent understaffing and degraded government services, a structural capacity problem that outlasts the current administration.
insights.quote: But the federal government's reputation as a stable employer is shot.

insights.claim: The Trump administration began momentous reductions in force in March 2025, removing thousands from agencies including Health and Human Services and Education, with the lawfulness of those cuts still working through the federal courts.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
entities: Department of Health and Human Services
insights.so_what: Establishes the ongoing scale of workforce reduction whose legal fate Slaughter now influences.

insights.claim: The Court's three-tier framework distinguishes principal officers (top-level, exercise significant authority, not supervised by another officer, require Senate confirmation), inferior officers (significant authority but supervised, appointable by department heads), and employees (no significant authority, governed by civil-service merit laws).
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: This is the framework Slaughter conspicuously avoided; knowing it clarifies exactly which categories remain legally uncertain after the ruling.

insights.claim: Independent agencies are defined less by falling outside the 15 cabinet departments than by whether their appointees have statutory for-cause removal protection, and they typically feature 3-7 member collective decision-making bodies rather than single heads.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Clarifies which agencies (multi-member, for-cause-protected) are the target class whose insulation Slaughter removes.

insights.claim: Bagner lists independent agencies with substantial market and policy reach whose leadership protections are affected—including the Consumer Product Safety Commission (product recalls), the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (electricity pricing), and the Merit Systems Protection Board (civil-servant removal adjudication).
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Identifies specific regulators touching consumer goods, energy pricing, and labor whose newfound political exposure could shift regulatory posture.
insights.quote: Federal Energy Regulatory Commission regulates electricity pricing

insights.claim: Cabinet secretaries serve at will and typically tender resignations as a formality at the end of a president's term, whereas independent-agency members historically held fixed terms that intentionally extended across administrations—a structural contrast Slaughter erodes.
insights.speaker: Nick Bagner, guest, law professor
insights.so_what: Highlights how the ruling pushes independent agencies toward the at-will, turnover-with-administration model of cabinet departments.
